title: Doomed to failure
subtitle: Our education system is designed to create failure - without it, there is no 'success'.
paragraphs: Here’s something I think all those involved in education policy need to understand.

There is no way, in our current system, for every child to be successful. Failure is built in. School exams are designed to differentiate between those who take them. Every year when 16-year-olds take GCSEs, some will do well and others will fail. This is by design. ‘Doing well’ at GCSE means, ‘better than most other people’. Doing ‘very well’ means ‘better than almost everyone else’. An exam on which everyone does well is not considered to be a good exam.

Not all tests are like this. The driving test, for example, is designed differently. It is a test of competency. Everyone could pass first time if they were all competent, and everyone would fail if they were all incompetent. It’s designed to make sure that those who pass are safe to drive, not to decide who is the best at driving.
GCSEs (and other high stakes exams) are not like this. They were designed to create successes and failures; to decide who is allowed to go onto further study and who is not.

This means that any rhetoric about ‘improving outcomes’ or ‘closing the attainment gap’ or ‘driving up standards’ betrays a lack of understanding of how school exams work. Failure is built into the system. The education system could not manage everyone doing well at GCSE, because it would then have no way to decide who can carry on to further academic study. They’d have to develop new tests to differentiate in other ways.
The only way to enable every young person to succeed would be to redesign the system so that success no longer means ‘did better than everyone else’.

If you measure everyone by an exam yardstick, there will always be failures. Anyone who tells you otherwise doesn’t understand how the system works.  And reforms and ‘improvements’ that don’t take this into account are doomed to failure in their turn.  Our young people need something better.
